coredata.dc:title: Experimental and theoretical studies on the photoelectrocatalytic property of titanium dioxide nanoarrays improved by S,N co-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wanli
Ou, Meigui
Yang, Chunlin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) were compounded with titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanorods by chemical surface modification. S,N-GQDs in diameter of 5 nm are uniformly distributed on the surface of TiO2 nanorod arrays. The presence of CS bonds in S,N-GQDs not only broaden the absorption of visible light by GQDs, but also enhanced the visible light absorption of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 composites. The photocurrent densities of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 were 3.66 times that of unmodified TiO2. The calculation results of density functional theory (DFT) showed that the band gap of S,N-GQDs is significantly reduced compared to GQDs, indicating that electrons are more likely to transit to higher energy levels and form more activation sites. Moreover, compared with TiO2, the DFT calculation results of band gap, density of states and charge density difference of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 showed that S,N-GQDs/TiO2 had a wide wavelength of visible light absorption and better electron-hole separation ability, resulting in the superior photoelectrocatalytic property. This research provides favorable analytical value for the development of photocatalysts based on heterostructures.

head.ce:title: Experimental and theoretical studies on the photoelectrocatalytic property of titanium dioxide nanoarrays improved by S,N co-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, sulfur and nitrogen co-doped graphene quantum dots (S,N-GQDs) were compounded with titanium dioxide (TiO2) nanorods by chemical surface modification. S,N-GQDs in diameter of 5 nm are uniformly distributed on the surface of TiO2 nanorod arrays. The presence of CS bonds in S,N-GQDs not only broaden the absorption of visible light by GQDs, but also enhanced the visible light absorption of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 composites. The photocurrent densities of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 were 3.66 times that of unmodified TiO2. The calculation results of density functional theory (DFT) showed that the band gap of S,N-GQDs is significantly reduced compared to GQDs, indicating that electrons are more likely to transit to higher energy levels and form more activation sites. Moreover, compared with TiO2, the DFT calculation results of band gap, density of states and charge density difference of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 showed that S,N-GQDs/TiO2 had a wide wavelength of visible light absorption and better electron-hole separation ability, resulting in the superior photoelectrocatalytic property. This research provides favorable analytical value for the development of photocatalysts based on heterostructures.

S,N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites with excellent photocatalytic activity were prepared by simple surface modification. N–H, C–N, CS functional groups of S,N doped GQDs is conducive to the absorption of visible light and the enhancement of catalytic activity. Both theoretical and experimental results confirmed the extension of optical absorption into the visible range and the frequent charge transfer from S,N-GQDs to the TiO2 surface, facilitating electron-hole separation and reducing charge recombination rate.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of society and industry, energy shortage has become one of the main problems faced by mankind in 21st century. The green and efficient new energy has become a research hotspot, among which hydrogen energy is a clean, efficient, safe and sustainable new energy, and its effective application can moderately alleviate the severe energy problem. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) hydrogen production using solar energy is regarded as the most promising technology for obtaining alternative energy, such as hydrogen [1–3]. However, how to more efficiently use solar energy to produce hydrogen remains a major challenge.

In the past few decades, many semiconductor materials, such as TiO2 [4], ZnO [5] WO3 [6], C3N4 [7], CdS [8], have been widely used as working electrode in PEC applications. Among these photocatalysts, TiO2 has the advantages of abundant reserves, low cost, and high stability and catalytic property [9,10], leading to its great application prospect in wastewater degradation, solar energy conversion, photocatalysis, etc [11–14]. Honda et al. first discovered that TiO2 can be used as a photoelectric anode in electrochemical cells to prepare hydrogen and oxygen by decomposing water under ultraviolet light [15]. In addition, María Tomás-Gamasa also reported that as a photosensitizer in photodynamic therapy, TiO2 can provide reactive oxygen species under light irradiation, to damage and destruction of targeted tumor cells [16]. However, the wide band gap (3.2 eV) in TiO2 electron structure makes it to absorb only sunlight in the ultraviolet region to a limited extent, and the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs limits the applications of TiO2 [17,18].

In order to widen the application of TiO2 under visible and near-infrared light in sunlight, inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs to enhance its catalytic activity, researchers have made a lot of attempts including metal ion doping, dye sensitization, heterojunction construction by narrow bandgap semiconductor composites [10,19–22]. Among them, the modification of TiO2 surface by graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is a very effective method [23–25], due to the excellent properties of GQDs, such as adjustable band gap, charge carrier selectivity, quantum confinement effect, and excellent fluorescence property [26–29]. GQDs can not only act as sensitizers to broaden the absorption range of TiO2, but the interaction between GQDs and TiO2 interface promotes the transfer of photogenerated electrons from TiO2 to GQDs, improves charge transfer efficiency, reduces the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, as to enhance the PEC property of TiO2 semiconductor-based photocatalysts [30,31]. A. Bayat et al. modified rutile TiO2 nanoflowers (TiO2 NFs) films with green photoluminescent GQDs, and the photocurrent of GQDs/TiO2 electrodes were enhanced by about 95 % more than the original TiO2 NFs [32].

Nevertheless, the light absorption of GQDs in the visible region is weak and the absorption region is limited, which can be effectively adjusted by ion or atom doping [33,34]. N,S doped GQDs with high electrocatalytic activity can be composited with other materials and made into electrode of batteries with high conductivity [35]. Yang et al. found that the S and N atoms in the graphene structure produced additional defects, providing more diffusion channels and electrochemically active sites, thereby improving the electrochemical property of the composites [36]. Therefore, doped GQDs can be used to form composite with TiO2, to broaden the excitation of TiO2 at the visible and near-infrared regions and to improve the charge separation of TiO2, so as to enhance the catalytic activity. However, the present research on the composites of GQDs and TiO2 have been based only on the experimental results, and the deep theoretical analysis by computational modeling is relatively lacking [37–39].

In this paper, S,N-GQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method and the effect of S,N co-doping on the optical properties of GQDs was studied. Subsequently, S,N-GQDs were compounded with TiO2 nanoarrays by simple spin coating to obtain S,N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites. The electron energy band, state density and charge density differentials of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 were simulated and calculated by density functional theory (DFT) based on the first principle, and the property enhancement mechanism of photocatalytic property of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 composites was further elaborated. This work provides a new way for the property optimization of TiO2-based nanocomposite photocatalysts, and a theoretical basis for the application of PEC hydrogen production.

Citric acid (AR, 99.5 %), thiourea (AR, 99 %), sodium hydroxide (AR≥96 %), titanium isopropoxide (TTIP, 95 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0–38.0 %), acetone, KOH(GR, 95 %). All chemicals were purchased commercially in Aladdin.

TiO2 nanorod arrays was prepared by hydrothermal method [40]. 15 ml HCl and 15 ml deionized water were mixed, and then 0.5 ml TTIP solution was added and stirred for 10 min to prepare the reaction solution. Transparent conductive glass of fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO, 4 × 1 cm) with acetone, ethanol and deionized water were ultrasonically cleaned 3 times, for 20 min each time. After being dried, FTO was placed in the above reaction solution in a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, with the conductive side facing down. The reactor was heated at 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling, FTO covered by formed TiO2 was removed and cleaned with alcohol and deionized water, followed by be dried and annealed at 500 °C in a muffle furnace for 1 h to obtain TiO2 nanorod arrays.
The preparation of GQDs was based on our previous research [41].

FTO dripped with 0.1 ml GQDs solution was first rotated at a low speed of 500 r/min for 10 s on a spin coate, and then at a speed of 1500 r/min for 50 s to obtain a more uniform and flat film. The spin-coated FTO was heated at 300 °C for 30 min to ensure the stable GQDs/TiO2 nanoarrays.

S,N-GQDs was prepared by hydrothermal method [42]. 1 g citric acid and 2 g thiourea were dissolved in 20 ml deionized water. The mixture was heated in a reactor at 160 °C for 6 h till the acquisition of a dark green solution, following by a centrifugation at a speed of 10,000 r/min for 15 min to obtain a uniformly dispersed solution of S,N-GQDs.
The synthesis process of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 is the same as the preparation of GQDs/TiO2 mentioned above. The overall preparation process is shown in Fig. 1 .

The microscopic morphologies of nanocomposites were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, SUPRA 40, Zeiss, Germany) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, USA). The crystal structure of the samples was determined by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, PANalytical B·V.) with a radiation source of Cu-Kα ray. The functional groups were recorded by fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was measured by an X-ray photoelectron spectrophotometer (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Fisher, USA) equipped with an Al Kα X-ray source (1486.6 eV) and the electron emission angle of 60°. The analysis area was 0.5 mm × 0.5 mm, and the vacuum degree was superior to 5.0 × 10−7 mBar, without etching and neutralizer. The sample was stored in closed polyethylene box prior to XPS analyses to avoid the surface exposure to carbon contamination [43]. The photoluminescence spectra were studied by fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent, USA). Light absorption spectra were analyzed by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-6300). Molecular spectra of vibrational and rotational energy level transitions of molecules were analyzed by Raman spectroscopy (Horiba, France).

The photocurrent density (J-V), transient photocurrent response (I-T) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were analyzed by the CHI660 electrochemical workstation of the three-electrode system. TiO2, GQDs/TiO2, S,N-GQDs/TiO2 electrodes were used as working electrodes, Ag/AgCl were used as reference electrodes, and Pt plate were used as counter electrodes. The electrolyte is 1.0 M KOH solution, and the Xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300) with a solar intensity (100 mW/cm2) is used as the light source. The photo-to-energy conversion efficiency of the electrode can be evaluated by the following formula [44]:
η= (I(1.23-V app))/P light
Where I is the optical current density measured at bias, V app is the external potential applied relative to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) (ERHE = E Ag/AgCl +0.0591ｘpH +0.1976(V)), and P light is the power density of light (100 mW/cm2).

DFT calculation was performed in the CASTEP module in Materials Studio based on a generalized gradient approximation (GGA) plane wave + Perdew Burke and Ernzerhof (PBE) function. The S,N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite system consists of TiO2(110) surface and S,N-GQDs plane containing 7 hexagonal rings. The geometry of the TiO2(110) surface was constructed from optimized rutile TiO2 unit cells containing a 4 × 2 surface model containing 96 O atoms and 48 Ti atoms. TiO2 is a strongly correlated system model with highly localized Ti3d orbital states and strong Coulomb interactions between the electrons that are often overlooked by standard DFT calculations [45]. Therefore, DFT + U was introduced for more accurate calculations with a U value of 7 eV, and a vertical vacuum space of 20 Å was added above TiO2 plane to avoid interactions between S,N-GQDs and TiO2. The ideal cut-off energy and all k-point grids in Brillouin were 300 eV and 3 × 2 × 2. The self-consistent field accuracy, interaction force, internal stress and tolerance deviation were set to 2.0 × 10−6 eV, 0.01 eV/Å, 0.02 GPa and 1.0 × 10−5 Å per atom, respectively.

Fig. 2 shows the SEM and TEM images of TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2. Fig. 2(a) and (b) show that TiO2 nanorods with a width of 100–150 nm are uniform in size and closely arranged. S,N-GQDs shown in Fig. 2(c) are spherical and distributed on the surface and gaps of the TiO2 nanorods [46], and it can be seen in Fig. 2(d) and (e) that the S,N-GQDs nanoparticles before and after recombination are spherical and have a size of about 5 nm. HRTEM image in Fig. 2(f) displays that the crystal plane spacing of S,N-GQDs and TiO2 are approximately 0.21 nm and 0.32 nm, corresponding to (1120) plane of graphene and (110) plane of TiO2,respectively. Energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 in Fig. 3 (a) indicates the main elements of C, O, S, N and Ti in the nanocomposites, and the inset shows the percentages of each element. EDS elemental mapping in Fig. 3(b–f) shows that S,N-GQDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of TiO2, which is conducive to the improvement of photocatalytic activity.

Fig. 4 (a) shows the XRD pattern of GQDs, S,N-GQDs, TiO2, GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2. The peaks at 2θ = 25–30° in GQDs and S,N-GQDs correspond to the diffraction of the graphite (002) plane [47]. The diffraction peaks at 35.9°, 62.6° and 69.6° correspond to (101), (002) and (112) planes of rutile phase TiO2 (JCPDS No: 21–1276), respectively. (002) planes of graphite at 26.2° appearing in GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 manifest that GQDs and S,N-GQDs were successfully compounded on TiO2 nanorod arrays. The Raman spectra of TiO2, GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 are shown in Fig. 4(b). The peaks at 445 and 609 cm−1 in TiO2 spectra belong to Eg and A1g modes of rutile phase TiO2 [48], respectively. Two peaks at 1396 and 1546 cm−1 are observed in the spectra of GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 correspond to the amorphous carbon (D-band) and ordered graphitic carbon (G-band) in graphene [49], indicating that GQDs, S,N-GQDs were successfully recombined on TiO2 nanorod arrays, which is consistent with the results of XRD (Fig. 4(a)).

Fig. 5 shows FTIR spectra of GQDs and S,N-GQDs. In addition to the CC bond, GQDs and S,N-GQDs are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups. The wide peak at 3306 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration peak of O–H bond, and the absorption peaks at 1635 and 2070 cm−1 correspond to the tensile vibration of CC bond and the in-plane bending vibration of CO, respectively. Compared with GQDs, additional functional groups appear in S,N-doped GQDs and a relatively small peak at 2984 cm−1 indicates the presence of N–H in S,N-GQDs. The peak at 1400, 858 and 1090 cm−1 correspond to the tensile vibration of the C–N, C–S and CS bonds, respectively, indicating the successful doping of the S,N atoms [50].

The chemical composition and surface groups of TiO2, GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 were further studied by XPS(Fig. 6 ). Aligning the spectrum with the C 1s peak of the adventitious carbon is not reliable as it can lead to non-physical results [51].

The binding energy (BE) of C1s peak can be corrected by subtracting the work function of TiO2 [52], i.e. 289.58 eV – ΦSA. The ΦSA value of rutile TiO2 is 5.07 eV, which was measured by ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) method [53]. Therefore, the corrected BE value of the C1s peak is 284.51 eV. Three prominent peaks at about 284, 459 and 529 eV in Fig. 6(a) belong to C1s, Ti 2p and O 1s, respectively, and the weak peaks of 164, 229 and 401 eV in the total spectrum of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 correspond to S 2p, S 2s and N 1s, respectively. The presence of a Si 2s peak at 150 eV and Si 2p peak at 100 eV may be attributed to adsorption in the air [54]. In the high-resolution spectrum of C1s in Fig. 6(b), five peaks at 284.51, 284.10, 285.03, 286.01 and 288.51 eV correspond to the sp2 hybrid carbon atoms C–C in the honeycomb lattice structure of GQDs, C–S–C, C–O, C–N and CO/Ti–O–C, respectively [55–57]. The peaks of O1s in Fig. 6(c) at 529.63, 530.08, 531.23 and 532.54 eV represent Ti–O–Ti bonds, Ti–O–C/CO, Ti–OH and C–O groups [58], and two peaks located at 458.41 and 464.11 eV in Fig. 6(d) correspond to Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively. Compared with TiO2, Ti2p and O1s peaks of GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 have a slight left shift, probably owing to the presence of C on the surface of the composite and its interaction with Ti and O [59]. The high-resolution N1s peak shown in Fig. 6(e) indicates the presence of pyridinic N (399.51 eV), pyrrolic N (400.31 eV) and graphite N (401.61 eV) in S,N-GQDs/TiO2, and therein the peak of pyrrolic N is the most intensive, followed by graphite N and pyridinic N. It has been reported that N-doping enhanced the catalytic activity of GQDs due to the increase in the active sites of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N [60]. The high-resolution S2p peak shown in Fig. 6(f) indicates that S,N-GQDs/TiO2 have C–S–C 2p3/2 (163.20 eV) and C–S–C 2p1/2 (164.40 eV) bonds [61]. The XPS results above show that the S,N atoms are successfully doped in GQDs/TiO2, and the composite contains a large number of functional groups, which is generally consistent with the FTIR results.

Fig. 7 (a) and (b) show the UV–visible absorption spectra of GQDs and S,N-GQDs, respectively. The absorption peaks at 360 nm correspond to the n-p* transition of the aromatic structure. S,N-GQDs have an extra strong absorption peak at 590 nm in the visible region in Fig. 7(b), which is related to the transition of n→π* of the conjugate bond CS [62]. The result consistent with the results of FTIR, indicates that S, N doping can improve the visible light absorption of GQDs. Fig. 7(c) shows the UV–visible absorption spectra of TiO2, GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2. After being compounded with GQDs, S,N-GQDs, the absorption intensity of TiO2 increases from 400 nm to 800 nm, indicating the increase of the light capture ability of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 in the visible region. Fig. 7(d) shows the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of TiO2, GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2. Compared with TiO2, GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 have lower PL, indicating that the combination of TiO2 with GQDs or S,N-GQDs can inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs to a greater extent [48].

In order to study the PEC property of TiO2 before and after recombination, a series of PEC property tests were carried out for the original TiO2, GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 electrodes. Fig. 8 (a) shows the photocurrent density measured by linear sweeping voltammetry (LSV). The photocurrent density of the original TiO2 is measured at 0.62 mA/cm2 at 1.23V vs. RHE, while the photocurrent density of GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 is significantly enhanced to 1.77 and 2.27 mA/cm2, which is 2.85 and 3.66 times that of TiO2, respectively. The improvement of PEC property of GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 is related to the increase of visible light absorption of GQDs. S,N-GQDs can absorb longer wavelengths of light to produce more photogenerated electrons, leading to higher the photocurrent density. Fig. 8(b) shows a typical transient photocurrent density-time curve measured at 1.23V vs. RHE bias, the light is turned on and off at intervals of 40 s. During 400 s alternating illumination, the prepared electrodes show good photosensitivity and stability. The maximum transient photocurrent density of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 obtained is 2.27 mA/cm2, which is 3.66 times higher than that of pristine TiO2. The electrons are driven from S,N-GQDs to TiO2 after the recombination, improving the effective separation of charges at the S,N-GQDs/TiO2 interface and enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. EIS measurement results shown in Fig. 8(c) help to understand more about the electron transport dynamics in the composites. The semicircle diameter in the Nyquist plot is related to the charge transfer resistance, the smaller semicircle diameter indicates a smaller charge transfer resistance, and a lower impedance resistance is conducive to generating a higher photocurrent [63]. The results in Fig. 8(c) show that the charge transfer resistance of GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 electrodes are significantly lower than TiO2 under illumination, indicating that the incorporation of GQDs, S,N-GQDs into the electrode structure enhances electron transfer and reduces charge recombination, thereby improving PEC efficiency. Fig. 8(d) shows the calculated photo-to-energy conversion efficiency according to LSV curves of the specimen. It is observed that the GQDs/TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 electrodes have maximum conversion efficiencies of 0.57 and 0.98 % at 0.72 and 0.65 V relative to RHE, respectively, which are 4.38 and 7.5 times of that of the original TiO2(0.13 % at 0.74 V vs RHE), respectively, proving the better potential of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 being used for the photoelectric hydrogen production.

In order to better understand the interface characteristics of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 and the influence mechanism of electronic structure on visible light absorption, charge separation efficiency and photocatalytic property, DFT simulation calculation was performed for the electron energy band structure of the composites. Fig. 9 (a) and (b) show the interface structure model of GQDs and S,N-GQDs, respectively, and Fig. 9(c) and (d) show the side view and top view of the S,N-GQDs/TiO2 composite structure. The structural model of S,N-GQDs is built up by taking GQDs as a reference, in which S or N atoms are doped at the edge and center of the C backbone [64]. S,N-GQDs/TiO2 are composited through the long-range van der Waals interactions between S,N-GQDs and TiO2 [65]. The calculated band gap of GQDs and is 2.072 and 0.068 eV, respectively (Fig. 10 (a) and (b)), and the smaller band gap of S,N-GQDs (compared to GQDs) means that electrons are more likely to transit to higher energy levels and form more activation sites, which is conducive to enhancing the photocatalytic property [66]. Fig. 10(c) shows that the calculated band gap of TiO2 is 2.669 eV and slightly smaller than the experimental band gap (3.0 eV) [67] for rutile TiO2 probably due to the self-interaction of GGA functional and PBE [65]. However, the band gap of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 is 0.319 eV (Fig. 10(d)), indicating that the band gap of TiO2 is significantly decreased after recombination, ascribed to the introduction of new impurity state in TiO2 after the combination with S,N-GQDs [68]. The decrease of the band gap is conducive to the redshift of the light absorption range, leading to that S,N-GQDs/TiO2 can absorb sunlight photons in a larger wavelength range which is consistent with the results of UV–visible absorption spectrum of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 in Fig. 7(c) [69].

To elucidate the electronic structure evolution of doped GQDs and composited TiO2, the total density of states (TDOS) and partial density of states (PDOS) were calculated and the results are shown in Fig. 11 . The density of states in Fig. 11(a) and (b) show that the TDOS of GQDs and S,N-GQDs are composed of s orbitals and p orbitals, indicating S,N doping does not change orbital composition of GQDs. However, compared with GQDs, the HOMO level of S,N-GQDs shifts to the right, probably attributed to the richer electrons in S and N atoms than in C atoms. The additional electrons induced by S,N doping can increase the HOMO energy of S,N-GQDs and lead to the transition of S,N-GQDs electrons [70]. Fig. 11(c) and (d) show the state densities of TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2, respectively. It can be seen that the valence band of TiO2 (Fig. 11(c)) is mainly composed of O2p states, and the proportion of Ti3d states is smaller, while the conduction band has more Ti3d states and the portions of O2p states is relatively small. The state density of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 (Fig. 11(d)) shows that the bonding states between −7 and −0.5 eV has mainly composed of p, d, s orbitals. The energy bands of the valence band are mainly controlled by C2p and O2p states, while the contribution of Ti3d states is small. There is overlap between C2p and Ti3d orbitals at the conduction band. Therefore, in S,N-GQDs/TiO2, the photoexcited electrons on S,N-GQDs, especially in the C2p state can be transferred to the Ti3d orbital at the conduction band and thus improve the photocatalytic efficiency.

The charge density difference between TiO2 and S,N-GQDs/TiO2 was calculated by DFT to indicate the charge redistribution and transfer of TiO2 after recombination, and the result is shown in Fig. 12 . The blue isosurfaces represents the charge accumulation region, and the yellow one represents the space charge depletion region. The charge density difference of TiO2 (Fig. 12(a)) shows that blue isosurfaces predominantly appear around O atoms gaining more electron density, and yellow isosurfaces appear around Ti atoms due to a loss of electron density. The yellow space region in S,N-GQDs/TiO2 (Fig. 12(b)) appears around the S,N-GQDs atoms, indicating that charge depletion occurs around the molecules of S,N-GQDs. The blue surface appears around the atoms at the TiO2 interface, indicating the accumulation of charge around the TiO2 atoms. Therefore, it is clear that the charge transfer mainly occurs through the S,N-GQDs and TiO2 interfaces. The above results show that the composite structure of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 significantly decrease the charge recombination of TiO2. The recombination of electron-hole pairs is effectively inhibited, resulting in a decrease in luminous intensity, which is consistent with the photoluminescence results of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 in Fig. 7(d).

In this paper, S,N-GQDs/TiO2 nanocomposites with excellent photocatalytic activity were prepared by a simple way of spin coating, and S,N-GQDs with an average size of 5 nm are uniformly distributed on the surface of TiO2 nanorod arrays. N–H, C–N, CS functional groups of S,N doped GQDs is conducive to the absorption of visible light and the enhancement of catalytic activity. Therefore, the experimental results show that S,N-GQDs/TiO2 have a visible light absorption intensity at wider range of wavelength, and the photocurrent density of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 is 1.28 and 3.66 times than that of GQDs/TiO2 and TiO2, respectively. DFT simulation results show that after being combined with S,N-GQDs, the electronic structure of TiO2 is introduced a new impurity state in the band gap, which significantly decreases the band gap of S,N-GQDs/TiO2, thereby extends the range of absorption spectra in the visible region. Compared with TiO2, the charge transfer ability of the composited interface of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 is improved, and the recombination of electron-hole pairs is effectively inhibited. The photoelectric conversion efficiency of S,N-GQDs/TiO2 as a catalyst is much higher than that of the original TiO2, which is expected to provide new opportunities in the preparation and application of hydrogen energy.

Wanli Zhang: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Conceptualization. Meigui Ou: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Chunlin Yang: Writing – review & editing, Investigation.
